title: Midnight Towboy

jokes.text: Don't let the bedbugs bite! Krusty's Anti-Bedbug Spray sold separately. May contain poison.
jokes.setup: Sweet bedtime routine with Krusty doll saying goodnight
jokes.punchline: Corporate disclaimer revealing the spray may be poisonous
jokes.explanation: Classic Simpsons corporate satire - takes innocent bedtime ritual and reveals capitalism's dark underbelly through children's toys. Perfect character integration with Krusty's brand cynicism.

jokes.text: Maggie singing 'Shout!' to stay awake instead of going to sleep
jokes.setup: Parents trying to put baby to sleep
jokes.punchline: Baby responds with energetic song about shouting
jokes.explanation: Solid situational irony - baby doing opposite of what parents want. Works through character but not particularly original.

jokes.text: My, you've grown. [Homer realizes he's holding adult Marge instead of baby Maggie]
jokes.setup: Homer absent-mindedly agrees to hold Maggie while Marge pours milk
jokes.punchline: Visual reveal that he's been holding his adult wife instead of the baby
jokes.explanation: Classic Homer obliviousness gag with perfect visual payoff. Economy is excellent - the line 'My, you've grown' does all the work.

jokes.text: Wha...?! Spilled milk? All over the floor.
jokes.setup: Homer has just made a mess by holding the wrong person
jokes.punchline: His matter-of-fact observation of the obvious disaster
jokes.explanation: Standard Homer dim-witted reaction. Gets a mild chuckle but nothing special.

jokes.text: There's a rumor you can mix milk, Mentos and Lotto scrapings to make jetpack fuel. Does it work? Kinda.
jokes.setup: Apu explains why he sold all his milk to teenagers
jokes.punchline: Ridiculous recipe that supposedly 'kinda' works
jokes.explanation: Wonderfully absurd Simpsons-style fake science. The casual 'kinda' makes it perfect. Great originality in combining random household items.

jokes.text: It's either milk or paint. It's paint. Or is it? Yeah, it's paint.
jokes.setup: Homer asks Moe for milk
jokes.punchline: Moe's characteristic uncertainty about his own inventory
jokes.explanation: Perfect Moe characterization - his seedy bar reality where he's unsure if substances are food or industrial materials. The back-and-forth builds nicely.

jokes.text: Well, then I'll have to charge you extra, depending on the color. Eggshell? No. Malabar ivory? No. Mediterranean ecru? No. Ah! Here it is...white.
jokes.setup: Moe decides to charge extra for paint by color
jokes.punchline: Elaborate paint color names that are all just 'white'
jokes.explanation: Great satire of paint industry marketing nonsense. The escalating fancy names for basic white paint is perfectly absurd and well-structured.

jokes.text: The blue ones are ads. That's the toolbar. Now you've opened Word; close it. Don't save it. Stop clicking. Don't go there! Why are you buying a freezer?
jokes.setup: Kids helping Marge navigate internet
jokes.punchline: Rapid escalation of computer disasters culminating in accidental purchase
jokes.explanation: Perfect escalation structure capturing universal computer frustration. Each step gets progressively worse.

jokes.text: Oh, I don't want to bother the Internet with my problem.
jokes.setup: Lisa suggests looking online for help with baby issues
jokes.punchline: Marge's polite concern about inconveniencing the entire Internet
jokes.explanation: Brilliant character moment - Marge's midwestern politeness applied to technology she doesn't understand. Perfect economy and highly quotable insight about generational tech gaps.

jokes.text: Creative Responses for Infant Edu-loving! The counselors can come day and night because they have no families themselves.
jokes.setup: Marge reads about a child-rearing service
jokes.punchline: Casual revelation that the counselors are lonely people with no lives
jokes.explanation: Dark humor about service industry workers. The casual cruelty in the marketing copy is very Simpsons - revealing societal callousness through corporate speak.

jokes.text: You just bought another freezer. Well, maybe I wanted another freezer!
jokes.setup: Marge accidentally making more online purchases
jokes.punchline: Marge defensively claiming she wanted the accidental purchase
jokes.explanation: Decent character beat showing Marge's defensive reaction to embarrassment.

jokes.text: At first I thought you were pointing to your crouch. You thought, huh? Yo, everybody! Get a load of 'Beautiful Mind' over here, with the thinkin'.
jokes.setup: Homer misunderstands Louie's gesture and mentions thinking
jokes.punchline: Louie mocks Homer for the concept of thinking itself
jokes.explanation: Good character establishment for Louie as anti-intellectual tough guy. The 'Beautiful Mind' reference works well as period-appropriate mockery of intelligence.

jokes.text: This don't mean nothing. [Louie being affectionate with his truck]
jokes.setup: Tough tow truck driver
jokes.punchline: Being tender/romantic with his truck while denying it means anything
jokes.explanation: Good character contradiction - tough guy being vulnerable while denying it.

jokes.text: I'm your boss! I'm sick of you! You're fired! No, you dumb Dominick. I'm my own boss.
jokes.setup: Homer thinks he figured out who Louie's boss is
jokes.punchline: Homer tries to fire Louie, revealing he misunderstood the self-employment concept
jokes.explanation: Classic Homer logic failure. His immediate power trip when he thinks he's the boss is perfectly in character. Solid but not exceptional.

jokes.text: She-atas, Hebrew canoes, spam cans, Swedish speedballs, and of course, stretch lame-os.
jokes.setup: Louie explains his nicknames for different types of cars
jokes.punchline: Series of ethnic stereotypes and puns for vehicle types
jokes.explanation: Mixed bag - some clever wordplay but relies on ethnic stereotypes. Character-appropriate for blue-collar guy but edges toward cheap humor territory.

jokes.text: Wow, you make people miserable and there's nothing they can do about it! Just like God.
jokes.setup: Homer admires Louie's power over frustrated customers
jokes.punchline: Compares tow truck driver authority to divine power
jokes.explanation: Excellent dark humor connecting everyday bureaucratic frustration to existential questions. Perfect economy and highly quotable. Very Simpsons in its casual blasphemy.

jokes.text: Flatbed Fred got killed in that murder-murder-murder-suicide.
jokes.setup: Louie explains job opening
jokes.punchline: Absurdly violent death involving three murders plus suicide
jokes.explanation: Dark comedy through escalation - the triple murder-suicide is absurdly excessive. Good economy in the delivery.

jokes.text: Man, you work as a silhouette model for one day and it haunts you for the rest of your life.
jokes.setup: Homer objects to sexy mudflaps being too risqué
jokes.punchline: Louie's explanation reveals he modeled for the mudflaps
jokes.explanation: Excellent character detail that recontextualizes the mudflap gag. The shame and regret in his voice makes it both funny and humanizing.

jokes.text: If I catch you on my turf, I'll rip off your head, vomit down your neck, pull out your heart, show it to your head, then shove 'em both down your neck hole, to which I previously alluded.
jokes.setup: Louie warns Homer about territory rules
jokes.punchline: Increasingly elaborate death threat ending with grammar correction
jokes.explanation: Great escalating threat that gets increasingly ridiculous, capped by unexpected grammatical self-awareness. The contrast between violence and vocabulary is perfect.

jokes.text: '...which I previously alluded...' Are there two 'L's' in 'alluded'?
jokes.setup: Louie just delivered an eloquent threat
jokes.punchline: Immediately second-guesses his spelling
jokes.explanation: Nice character beat showing Louie's insecurity about his intelligence despite using big words. Works as immediate callback to previous line.

jokes.text: Oh hello-o! Hello! Hello, Captain Hook!
jokes.setup: Homer points his tow hook the wrong way
jokes.punchline: Louie's reaction to the obvious Captain Hook visual
jokes.explanation: Obvious visual pun. Gets the job done but nothing special - the Captain Hook reference is the most predictable possible joke.

jokes.text: Really? What's your handicap? Pogonophobia--fear of beards and mustaches.
jokes.setup: Person claims entitlement to handicapped parking
jokes.punchline: Ridiculous phobia as disability
jokes.explanation: Decent absurdist humor with made-up phobia. Structure is solid but the concept isn't deeply integrated with character or show themes.

jokes.text: Stay back! All of ya! [Fear reaction to beards]
jokes.setup: Man with beard phobia sees bearded people
jokes.punchline: Physical comedy reaction to his stated fear
jokes.explanation: Logical follow-through on the phobia joke. Shows commitment to the bit but doesn't add much beyond the initial concept.

jokes.text: Marge, your code pendent hyper-nurturing has infantilized your baby. You've made her queen of her 'clingdom.'
jokes.setup: Professional child counselor diagnoses Marge's parenting
jokes.punchline: Made-up psychology terms including 'clingdom' pun
jokes.explanation: Good satire of pop psychology jargon. 'Clingdom' is a solid pun that captures both the problem and the professional's pretentiousness.

jokes.text: Allow me to demonstri-care. Actualize!
jokes.setup: Counselor promises to show rather than tell
jokes.punchline: Made-up verb 'demonstri-care' combining demonstration with caring
jokes.explanation: Creative wordplay that perfectly captures New Age parenting speak. Good economy and character consistency.

jokes.text: She's not crying, She's celebrating her independence. Don't crash that party.
jokes.setup: Baby clearly distressed and crying
jokes.punchline: Counselor reframing obvious distress as celebration
jokes.explanation: Good satire of toxic positivity in childcare. 'Don't crash that party' is particularly absurd.

jokes.text: hugs are drugs, and your daughter's an addict.
jokes.setup: Marge wants to comfort crying Maggie with a hug
jokes.punchline: Counselor frames affection as addiction
jokes.explanation: Excellent satirical take on extreme parenting advice. Perfect economy and highly quotable. Dark humor in pathologizing normal parent-child affection.

jokes.text: Now her childhood can begin-ualize.
jokes.setup: Counselor explains benefits of independence
jokes.punchline: Another made-up therapeutic verb
jokes.explanation: Continues the pattern of made-up therapeutic language. Less impact than earlier examples but maintains character consistency.

jokes.text: I've never done anything like that before. Yeah, well, I'm glad your first time was such a success.
jokes.setup: Marge apologizing for kicking counselor
jokes.punchline: Counselor's response sounds sexual about her 'first time'
jokes.explanation: Low-hanging innuendo. Works minimally but relies on cheap double entendre.

jokes.text: I've got tow dough I'm looking to blow, Moe.
jokes.setup: Homer has money from towing and wants to spend it
jokes.punchline: Rhyming slang 'tow dough' for towing money
jokes.explanation: Simple but effective rhyming wordplay. Shows Homer's pride in his new profession through linguistic play.

jokes.text: Four 'Lobster-politans' comin' up.
jokes.setup: Homer ordering fanciest drinks
jokes.punchline: Moe's ridiculous fusion of lobster and cosmopolitan
jokes.explanation: Good Moe character moment - his attempt at upscale drinks is perfectly gauche.

jokes.text: That's a one-hour parking zone, and you've been here a good 81 minutes. Correction: a great 81 minutes.
jokes.setup: Homer points out parking violation to his friend
jokes.punchline: Barney's positive reframe of the time spent with friends
jokes.explanation: Nice character moment for Barney showing his simple optimism. The 'correction' format works well structurally.

jokes.text: Huh, good joke, Homer. You can unhook my car now. Uh, looks like he's driving away with it.
jokes.setup: Barney thinks Homer is joking about towing
jokes.punchline: Realization that Homer is actually stealing his car
jokes.explanation: Good escalation showing consequences of Homer's job affecting friendships. The denial-reality gap works well.

jokes.text: Marge, when you're married to a midnight towboy, you might not see him for days on end.
jokes.setup: Marge asks where Homer has been
jokes.punchline: Homer's dramatic romanticizing of tow truck driving
jokes.explanation: Perfect Homer delusion - elevating mundane job to romantic ideal. 'Midnight towboy' makes it sound like a Western or noir profession.

jokes.text: Marge, when you're married to a midnight towboy, you might not see him for days on end.
jokes.setup: Marge asking where Homer's been
jokes.punchline: Homer treating tow truck driving like being a cowboy or outlaw
jokes.explanation: Great character delusion - Homer romanticizing mundane job. 'Midnight towboy' is perfectly grandiose.

jokes.text: Hey, when you married a man who would, years later, without warning, become a tow truck driver, you knew what the deal would eventually be.
jokes.setup: Marge objects to his absence
jokes.punchline: Homer's illogical defense using impossible foresight
jokes.explanation: Brilliant absurdist logic that's perfectly Homer. The circular reasoning about predicting unpredictable career changes is hilariously twisted yet delivered with complete confidence.

jokes.text: You can't impound my spirit.
jokes.setup: Skinner's car has been towed and he has no transportation
jokes.punchline: His defiant but ultimately useless proclamation while riding a child's scooter
jokes.explanation: Perfect Skinner - maintaining dignity in absurd circumstances. The contrast between noble sentiment and ridiculous situation works well.

jokes.text: Top of the mornin', Tow-mer. It's Homer, idiot. So it is.
jokes.setup: Willie greets Homer with pun on his name
jokes.punchline: Homer's correction followed by Willie's casual acceptance
jokes.explanation: Simple name pun. Willie's casual 'so it is' response is in character but the overall bit is fairly predictable.

jokes.text: I guess I'm more powerful than God now.
jokes.setup: Homer towed the Sunday School bus with kids still inside
jokes.punchline: Homer's conclusion about surpassing divine power
jokes.explanation: Great callback to earlier 'just like God' joke with escalation. Homer's casual blasphemy while describing child endangerment is darkly perfect.

jokes.text: You know what they say: 'With great power comes great responsibility.' Who said that?! I'll kill them with my power.
jokes.setup: Homer quotes Spider-Man philosophy
jokes.punchline: Immediately threatens violence against whoever said it
jokes.explanation: Perfect subversion of classic moral lesson. Homer's immediate violent rejection of responsibility is hilariously in character and shows complete misunderstanding of the wisdom.

jokes.text: You know what they say: 'With great power comes great responsibility.' Who said that?! I'll kill them with my power.
jokes.setup: Homer quoting Spider-Man's famous line
jokes.punchline: Completely missing the point and threatening to murder the quote's originator
jokes.explanation: Brilliant subversion of famous quote. Homer's complete misunderstanding and violent response is perfect character comedy.

jokes.text: Take that, you tow-talitarian. Doesn't that joke make light of totalitarians? Stalin put my grandmother in a forced labor camp for 20 years.
jokes.setup: Comic Book Guy makes pun about Homer's towing authority
jokes.punchline: Immediate serious objection to his own joke's implications
jokes.explanation: Brilliant meta-humor where character critiques his own joke for historical insensitivity. Perfect Comic Book Guy pedantry applied to his own wordplay. Very sophisticated comedy writing.

jokes.text: Take that, you tow-talitarian. Doesn't that joke make light of totalitarians? Stalin put my grandmother in a forced labor camp for 20 years.
jokes.setup: Mayor making pun about Homer's authoritarianism
jokes.punchline: Comic Book Guy's overly serious historical objection
jokes.explanation: Good character moment - Comic Book Guy being overly literal about historical references. Dark turn works well.

jokes.text: Look, I was insensitive, and I'm sorry. But what this is really about is a bully with a winch.
jokes.setup: Mayor apologizing for insensitive joke
jokes.punchline: Reducing complex situation to 'bully with a winch'
jokes.explanation: Good phrase that captures the situation succinctly. The alliteration helps make it memorable.

jokes.text: If I'd wanted the laws of this town enforced to the letter, I wouldn't have hired fatty here as police chief.
jokes.setup: Mayor complains about Homer's overzealous towing
jokes.punchline: Casual insult about Chief Wiggum's incompetence being intentional
jokes.explanation: Good political corruption joke - Mayor admitting he hired incompetent police chief on purpose. Shows the cynical reality behind Springfield's dysfunction.

jokes.text: Hear that, Ralphie? The mayor knows daddy.
jokes.setup: Mayor insulting Wiggum in front of his son
jokes.punchline: Wiggum focusing on recognition rather than insult
jokes.explanation: Perfect Wiggum obliviousness. Missing the insult and focusing on positive aspect is very in character.

jokes.text: Theater tickets, huh? Now ya got nothin'.
jokes.setup: Someone complains about missing theater due to car being towed
jokes.punchline: Louie's cruel satisfaction in ruining cultural activities
jokes.explanation: Good character beat showing Louie's sadistic pleasure in others' misfortune. The economy is excellent but the concept is straightforward.

jokes.text: At least you need me, sack of potatoes.
jokes.setup: Marge feels unneeded as Maggie becomes independent
jokes.punchline: Addressing a literal sack of potatoes for companionship
jokes.explanation: Touching and funny moment of maternal desperation. The specificity of 'sack of potatoes' makes it more absurd than generic 'talking to objects' gag.

jokes.text: multiple tickets, handicapped spot, parked by a hydrant, incomprehensible vanity plate. If my life as a tow truck driver were a teleplay, this would be the end of act two.
jokes.setup: Homer discovers the ultimate parking violation
jokes.punchline: Breaking fourth wall to comment on dramatic structure
jokes.explanation: Sophisticated meta-humor where Homer recognizes his life following dramatic structure. The escalating parking violations build to perfect climax awareness.

jokes.text: Okay, I made a mistake. Why don't you give me two demerit points in a point system we work out right now?
jokes.setup: Homer is caught in Louie's territory and in danger
jokes.punchline: Desperately tries to negotiate bureaucratic solution to physical threat
jokes.explanation: Perfect Homer logic - trying to solve immediate physical danger through improvised bureaucracy. Shows his faith in systems over reality.

jokes.text: Oh, a trap door. What's down there? Your Meatball collection?
jokes.setup: Homer falls through trap door in tough guy's truck
jokes.punchline: Random guess about meatball collection below
jokes.explanation: Random absurdist humor typical of Homer's free-associating mind. 'Meatball collection' is oddly specific and unexpectedly innocent for the threatening situation.

jokes.text: We're just like you. Tow truck drivers who got greedy. Actually, we're an a cappella group and we're gonna sing 'Under the BoardWalk.' The truth is, we're just waiting for each others' hair to grow so we can braid it.
jokes.setup: Homer asks who the other prisoners are
jokes.punchline: Series of increasingly absurd explanations for their captivity
jokes.explanation: Great escalating absurdity from serious to ridiculous to weirdly intimate. Each explanation gets more unexpected and the hair braiding is perfectly bizarre.

jokes.text: It sure is great not having Homer around to tell us where we can or can't park. Yeah, without the crushing rule of law, society will do a better job of regulating itself.
jokes.setup: Carl celebrates Homer's absence and parking freedom
jokes.punchline: Lenny's overly intellectual analysis of anarchism
jokes.explanation: Smart political satire disguised as casual conversation. Lenny's unexpectedly sophisticated political theory contrasts perfectly with the mundane parking context.

jokes.text: [Visual chaos of cars colliding and parking disasters]
jokes.setup: Lenny's theory about society self-regulating without law enforcement
jokes.punchline: Immediate visual proof of the opposite with traffic chaos
jokes.explanation: Perfect visual punchline to political theory setup. Shows rather than tells why law enforcement exists. Excellent economy and timing.

jokes.text: Ask no questions. And do not call the police, or I will kill me.
jokes.setup: Bart asks where Homer is calling from
jokes.punchline: Homer threatens suicide rather than involving police
jokes.explanation: Dark absurdist humor in Homer's confused priorities. Threatening self-harm to avoid police involvement shows his backwards understanding of protection.

jokes.text: Avast, ye sky whale!
jokes.setup: Traffic chaos with cars flying through the air
jokes.punchline: Sea Captain treating flying car as maritime threat
jokes.explanation: Perfect Sea Captain line - applying naval terminology to absurd land situation. 'Sky whale' for flying car is brilliantly in character.

jokes.text: Once upon a time, there was a hilarious ogre named Shrek Three. He lived in medieval times, but sometimes he'd say things from today times...
jokes.setup: Another story to pass time
jokes.punchline: Analyzing Shrek's anachronistic humor formula
jokes.explanation: Good meta-commentary on animated movie humor tropes. 'Today times' is charmingly awkward phrasing that captures the analytical approach.

jokes.text: We can say these swears any time we want because they're in the Bible! I don't think 'Leviticus' is a swear. Shut the hell up, you damn ass whore.
jokes.setup: Bart claims biblical words are swears
jokes.punchline: Lisa's correction followed by actual swearing
jokes.explanation: Good escalation from innocent misunderstanding to actual profanity. Lisa's pedantic correction followed by Bart's real swears works well structurally.

jokes.text: We can say these swears any time we want because they're in the Bible! I don't think 'Leviticus' is a swear. Shut the hell up, you damn ass whore.
jokes.setup: Bart claiming biblical words are acceptable swears
jokes.punchline: Lisa's correction followed by Bart's actual profanity
jokes.explanation: Good sibling dynamic. Lisa's correction and Bart's response are perfectly in character.

jokes.text: Well, I know he seems to get dumber every year, but lately he's plateaued.
jokes.setup: Marge worries about Homer getting dumber
jokes.punchline: Bart's scientific analysis suggesting Homer's stupidity has leveled off
jokes.explanation: Brilliant analysis of Homer's character arc using scientific terminology. 'Plateaued' is perfect word choice suggesting Bart has been tracking this data over time.

jokes.text: And if elected president of our small community, I will determine once and for all how far we can go without being gay.
jokes.setup: Political speech from captive tow truck driver
jokes.punchline: Bizarre campaign promise about masculinity boundaries
jokes.explanation: Absurdist political satire about masculine anxiety. The formal political language applied to ridiculous premise works but edges toward cheap shock humor.

jokes.text: And to pay you back, I'm returning your nose. Here's your nose. Daddy's putting back your nose. That's your--Oh, wait. That's Lisa's nose.
jokes.setup: Homer gratefully reuniting with Maggie
jokes.punchline: Confusion about whose nose belongs to whom
jokes.explanation: Standard physical comedy bit with baby nose confusion. Gets a chuckle but relies on familiar baby-game humor. The escalation to wrong nose adds something.

jokes.text: Are you sure? Hugs are dr... [Marge cuts off CRIE counselor mid-sentence to hug Maggie]
jokes.setup: Counselor tries to repeat anti-hug philosophy
jokes.punchline: Marge interrupts by doing exactly what he's warning against
jokes.explanation: Good callback to earlier 'hugs are drugs' line with physical comedy payoff. Marge's maternal instincts overriding expert advice makes a satisfying character moment.

tmdb_overview: Homer takes a job as a tow-truck driver, and Marge hires a service that claims to make babies less clingy. Both decisions have unexpected consequences.
